topic: Someone discovers their mother kept every school assignment
raw_output:
[story 1]

The cardboard box, tucked deep in the attic, smelled faintly of old paper and lavender. Liam pulled it down, his fingers tracing the rough edges of the packing tape. Inside, stacked neatly, were binders and folders, each labeled with a precise, looping script. Every single assignment from middle school, from that awkward geometry test to the sprawling history essays, was accounted for. A cold knot tightened in his chest as he lifted one, the crisp pages a stark contrast to the usual disorganized chaos of his own desk. He flipped through a stack, his breath catching in his throat. It was meticulous. It was exhaustive. A wave of profound disappointment washed over him, leaving a hollow ache behind his ribs. He dropped the folder back into the box, the sound muffled by the dust motes dancing in the single shaft of light cutting through the gloom.

[story 2]

"Mom, what is all this?" Sarah asked, her voice tight with suspicion as she surveyed the overflowing filing cabinet. Her mother, usually so breezy, was meticulously organizing stacks of graded papers, each one perfectly preserved in clear plastic sleeves. Sarah felt a sudden, sharp withdrawal. She’d always assumed her mother was forgetful, scatterbrained, but this level of preservation felt deliberate, almost performative. When her mother finally looked up, her expression was unreadable, a smooth, unyielding surface. Sarah didn't say anything; she just turned away, the movement stiff, and walked towards the hallway, her steps uneven, as if the floorboards themselves were suddenly unsteady beneath her.

[story 3]

I found the box behind the linen closet. It wasn't grand; just a plain brown container. But when I opened it, the sheer volume of completed work—every homework sheet, every project rubric, every graded paper—was overwhelming. My hands started to tremble, the paper feeling flimsy and fragile under my touch. I picked up a science report, the ink slightly faded, and stared at the neat handwriting. A tightness built behind my eyes, a pressure that made focusing on anything else impossible. I didn't want to look at it anymore. Instead, I shoved the box back onto the shelf, my shoulders slumping forward, the weight of the expectation suddenly feeling too heavy to bear.
